model_info.title: About the models
model_info.intro: A weather forecast is a model computation, not a fact. Over Georgia's complex terrain different models often disagree about the very same point. When all four say the same thing, the forecast is trustworthy; when they diverge, be skeptical and check the latest run.

ecmwf.provider: European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF), UK.
ecmwf.resolution: Native grid ~9 km; open data via Open-Meteo comes on a 0.25° grid (~25 km).
ecmwf.updates: 2 main runs per day (00 and 12 UTC), horizon ~10 days.
ecmwf.strengths: Widely regarded as the most accurate global model — it has led verification scores in the 3–10 day range for years. Excellent at large-scale dynamics: fronts, Black Sea cyclones, temperature waves.
ecmwf.limits: The global grid smooths the Caucasus — local valley and mountain effects (foehn, temperature inversions, narrow-valley rain) get averaged away.
ecmwf.georgia: A reliable base for the big picture: when a front arrives, where temperature is heading. Exact precipitation placement in the mountains is less trustworthy — compare with a high-resolution model there.

icon_eu.resolution: ~6.5 km — the highest of the four.
icon_eu.updates: 4 times per day (00/06/12/18 UTC), horizon ~5 days.
icon_eu.strengths: High resolution pays off over complex terrain: precipitation placement, mountain–valley contrasts and convective (afternoon) showers come out better than in global models.
icon_eu.limits: Short horizon (~120 hours). Georgia sits near the eastern edge of its European domain, and quality degrades slightly near the edge.
icon_eu.georgia: Often the best pick for a detailed 2–4 day forecast, especially in mountain regions — Svaneti, Kazbegi, Tusheti.

gfs.updates: 4 times per day, horizon up to 16 days — the longest.
gfs.strengths: Frequent updates and a long horizon make it good for spotting trends. The data is fully open and free, which is why most weather apps worldwide run on it.
gfs.limits: Usually trails ECMWF in verification. Over complex terrain it tends to overdo or misplace precipitation; valley temperatures are less accurate.
gfs.georgia: Use it as a third opinion and for the beyond-7-days trend (warming or cooling), not for the details of a specific day.

yr.resolution: Point-based — not a separate model but a post-processed forecast.
yr.updates: Hourly steps for ~2.5 days, then 6-hour steps to ~9 days.
yr.strengths: Applies its own post-processing on top of ECMWF: corrects temperature to the point's real altitude and blends in local statistics. A ready, polished answer for a specific place.
yr.limits: A black box — you can't see what the correction changed. Precipitation distribution in the mountains still rests on a global basis.
yr.georgia: A strong independent reference. Its temperature in mountainous places is often the most accurate thanks to the altitude correction.
